speaker: POWELL
text: over time. We are also continuing to reduce our securities holdings. Looking ahead, we'll

speaker: POWELL
text: take a data-dependent approach in determining the extent to which additional policy-firming

speaker: POWELL
text: may be appropriate. I will have more to say about today's monetary policy actions after

speaker: POWELL
text: briefly reviewing economic developments. The U.S. economy slowed significantly last year,

speaker: POWELL
text: with real GDP rising at a below trend pace of 0.9%. The pace of economic growth in the

speaker: POWELL
text: first quarter of this year continued to be modest at 1.1%, despite a pickup in consumer

speaker: POWELL
text: spending. Activity in the housing sector remains weak, largely reflecting higher mortgage

speaker: POWELL
text: rates. Higher interest rates and slower output growth also appear to be weighing on business

speaker: POWELL
text: fixed investment. The labor market remains very tight. Over the first three months of

speaker: POWELL
text: the year, job gains averaged 345,000 jobs per month. The unemployment rate remained very

speaker: POWELL
text: low in March at 3.5%. Even so, there are some signs that supply and demand in the labor

speaker: POWELL
text: market are coming back into better balance. The labor force participation rate has moved

speaker: POWELL
text: up in recent months, particularly for individuals aged 25 to 54 years. Nominal wage growth has

speaker: POWELL
text: shown some signs of easing, and job vacancies have declined so far this year. But overall,

speaker: POWELL
text: labor demand still substantially exceeds the supply of available workers.

speaker: POWELL
text: Inflation remains well above our longer-run goal of 2%. Over the 12 months ending in March,

speaker: POWELL
text: available PCE prices rose 4.2%. Excluding the volatile food and energy categories, core

speaker: POWELL
text: PCE prices rose 4.6%. Inflation has moderated somewhat since the middle of last year. Nonetheless,

speaker: POWELL
text: inflation pressures continue to run high, and the process of getting inflation back down

speaker: POWELL
text: to 2% has a long way to go. Despite elevated inflation, longer-term inflation expectations

speaker: POWELL
text: appear to remain well anchored as reflected in a broad range of surveys of households,

speaker: POWELL
text: businesses, and forecasters, as well as measures from financial markets.

speaker: POWELL
text: The Fed's monetary policy actions are guided by our mandate to promote maximum employment

speaker: POWELL
text: and stable prices for the American people. My colleagues and I are acutely aware that high

speaker: POWELL
text: inflation imposes significant hardship as it erodes purchasing power, especially for those

speaker: POWELL
text: least able to meet the higher costs of essentials, like food, housing, and transportation.

speaker: POWELL
text: We are highly attentive to the risks that high inflation poses to both sides of our mandate,

speaker: POWELL
text: and we are strongly committed to returning inflation to our 2% objective. At today's meeting,

speaker: POWELL
text: the Committee raised the target range for the federal funds rate by a quarter percentage

speaker: POWELL
text: point, bringing the target range to five to five and a quarter percent, and we're continuing

speaker: POWELL
text: to the process of significantly reducing our securities holdings.

speaker: POWELL
text: With today's action, we have raised interest rates by five percentage points and a little

speaker: POWELL
text: more than a year. We are seeing the effects of our policy tightening on demand in the most

speaker: POWELL
text: interest rates sensitive.
